title: SuperBEST v3 Cost Analysis — Biology Block 1: Exponential Models

eq1_exponential_growth.name: Exponential population growth
eq1_exponential_growth.notes: Standard exponential growth. Three operators: mul, exp, mul.

eq2_exponential_decay.name: Exponential decay
eq2_exponential_decay.notes: Growth plus one negation operator (cost 2). Costs 2 more nodes than Eq 1 solely due to the sign flip.

eq3_radioactive_decay.name: Radioactive decay
eq3_radioactive_decay.notes: STRUCTURALLY IDENTICAL to Eq 2. Same operator tree, different parameter names (λ vs r with negation). Node count identical: 7. This is a domain rename, not a distinct computation.

eq4_doubling_time.name: Doubling time
eq4_doubling_time.notes: ln(2) ≈ 0.6931 is a free constant terminal. Only two operators needed: recip + mul. The informal '0 nodes' in the problem statement refers to no complex subexpressions beyond the ratio of two terminals — the actual SuperBEST cost is 4 (recip + mul are still operators).

eq5_half_life.name: Half-life
eq5_half_life.notes: STRUCTURALLY IDENTICAL to Eq 4 (doubling time). Parameter λ vs r, same operator tree. Node count: 4.

eq6_carbon14_dating.name: Carbon-14 dating

steps: sub(ln(N₀), ln(N))   → 2 nodes  [log subtraction = log ratio]

eq6_carbon14_dating.notes: Two viable trees. Option B (log difference) saves 1 node vs Option A (direct ratio). The sub operator costs 2, same as recip, but avoids an extra recip+mul pair for the inner division. Best cost: 8 nodes.

eq7_continuous_compounding.name: Continuous compounding (finance/biology)
eq7_continuous_compounding.notes: STRUCTURALLY IDENTICAL to Eq 1 (exponential population growth). Domain label changes (V₀ vs N₀, financial vs biological context) but operator tree is the same. Node count: 5.

eq8_cell_growth_rate.name: Cell growth rate from doubling time
eq8_cell_growth_rate.notes: STRUCTURALLY IDENTICAL to Eq 4 and Eq 5. Same operator tree, just solving for r instead of t. Node count: 4.

summary_table.name: Cell growth rate from doubling time

class_A_growth.insight: Positive-exponent exponential. Two multiplications + one exp = 5 nodes. The most basic exponential template.

class_B_decay.insight: Decay = growth + negation. The neg operator costs 2 extra nodes. Radioactive and biological decay are computationally identical — different physical domains, same operator tree.

class_C_log_ratio.insight: Ratio of a free constant to a free parameter. Cheapest non-trivial formula: only recip + mul. ln(2) contributes 0 nodes as a mathematical constant terminal. Three biology/biophysics formulas collapse to this single 4-node template.

class_D_log_difference_ratio.insight: Most complex formula in this block. Requires two log evaluations, a subtraction, a reciprocal, and a multiplication. Optimal tree uses log-difference (sub) rather than log-of-ratio (which would cost 9 nodes via inner recip+mul for A/B). The sub operator at cost 2 is the same price as recip, making log-difference the winner.

key_observations: All 8 equations reduce to 4 distinct operator tree templates (classes A, B, C, D).
Growth vs decay differ by exactly 2 nodes: one neg operator (cost 2). This is the minimal computational signature of sign direction in an exponent.
Three apparently different biological formulas (doubling time, half-life, cell growth rate) are computationally identical 4-node trees — they are the same computation.
Eqs 1 and 7 (population growth vs compound interest) are structurally identical: biology and finance share the same 5-node template.
Eqs 2 and 3 (exponential decay vs radioactive decay) are structurally identical: same tree across physical domains.
Carbon-14 dating (Eq 6) is the only formula requiring two log evaluations; it is the costliest at 8 nodes. Optimal via log-difference (sub), not log-of-ratio (inner mul+recip would cost 9).
The informal '0 nodes' comment in Eqs 4 and 8 means no complex nested subexpressions — the actual SuperBEST count is 4 (recip + mul are real operator nodes).
Node range across this block: 4 (min, class C) to 8 (max, class D). A narrow spread of 4 nodes across all 8 biology equations — exponential models are structurally simple.
